speaker: interviewer
text: The date is April 2nd, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about how you came to this country and what year that was.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Israel. I was a sergeant major in the Israeli army. I was very proud to be in the Israeli army, and I liked it. What happened was really I had a fiance who was very, very religious. And I'd dated him for two years. And then he wanted me to become more religious. And I was hoping he would become less religious. And so we-- I realized that that was not going to work out. And so we broke up. He got married. And he kept visiting me in Tel Aviv on the weekend, when I would have a date there. And he got rid of one of my dates. I went to the kitchen to fix coffee. I came back. I said, where is-- so and so. Well, I talked to him.  He doesn't have any serious intentions. I said, I didn't know you were my guardian. He said, well, I feel responsible. I said, you shouldn't feel responsible. But the truth was that he still loved me, and I still loved him. Otherwise I could've kicked him out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Second time it happened, I got a little bit mad. And I said, well, I guess I will have to go far away, where you cannot come and interfere with my private life. And as things went, I-- I was very good friends with a family, very prominent family in Tel Aviv. She's Hungarian, actually Czechoslovakian-- Sarah Kor. And they had a wonderful family. And they kept telling me that my-- that her brother-in-law is coming from United States, that he's a survivor, and would I be interested in meeting him? I said, OK. I mean, what do I care? I meet one more guy. And they were really very pushing that shidduch very much. And I really had a good time with them. He was a journalist, editor of [INAUDIBLE]. He would take me to all the plays, and kind of fun. And I liked the company.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my husband arrived. And [{PAUSES] I knew everything about him, I think. He's very short. And it was Passover, 1960. He was skinny, short, kind of nice, but nothing particular. I mean, we were at the seder at my sister-in-law's house. And his behavior was very strange. He-- every place where he should have been serious he broke into laughter. I found myself very uncomfortable. And so I told my sister-in-law next day that I really was not interested in seeing him anymore. I cannot relate to somebody acting that way, that he might be very nice guy, but I find that my instincts tell me that I should run the other direction.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so she called me a few days later. She said, well, you've got to take a vacation. We are going to go to Eilat with him. And actually my husband and I cannot make it, so we don't want him to go alone with a cab driver. It's a long trip. And you have never been to Eilat, so why don't you just go? I said-- I spoke no English. He spoke no Hebrew. We had two dictionaries. And it was wild. I mean, how on earth can you communicate? Yes, looking up in the dictionary. [{PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: We got along a little bit better. He had good sense of humor. He loved to play the piano. And he was-- he just plays the piano by ear. And he liked to dance. And we went dancing. And then he said to me, will you marry me, 10 days after I meet him. I said, no. I said, I'll tell  you what. Let's get engaged, and you write to me letters, and I'll write you letters. He said, no, [{PAUSES] or now or never. My sister was already married. And she just had a baby. And my aunt who raised us said, in our family, there are no old maids. [{PAUSES] She said, get married and then if you don't like it, get divorced. That's better. There was a lot of pressure put by many people on me, get married, get married, get married. And I broke up already three engagements. That was not-- [INAUDIBLE]. If there was nothing to go, and I really wasn't feeling like I wanted to get married. I enjoyed being single. And I didn't need to get married. But after I think the fourth-- fourth or fifth time, I said, OK, let's get married. And it wasn't really me. And I was not crazy in love with him. It was the circumstances that I wanted to get away from Israel. I wanted to get away from that old boyfriend. And I wanted everybody to stop, leave me alone. And I said, well, you know, it will be exciting to go to America. So I got married. He came back to the United States. I worked in Israel. I still was a sergeant major in  the Israeli Army and I needed to get my release from the army-- which took me quite a while-- as well as a visa, entry visa in United States. And there was one other problem, that my poor sister was married, but she was having a very difficult time. While I lived in Israel, I helped them financially a great deal. I was almost like a mother to her. I mothered her very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I had a very good salary. And I would help her with-- and my brother-in-law, we found out at that time that he was injured in the Independence War. And he had epilepsy. And he could not accept the fact that he had epilepsy. And he could not work. So my sister was a nurse, working with a baby. And I could not watch that very well. And that really was a very big problem for me. And I said, I'd rather go far away. I don't want to watch it. I don't know how to deal with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I came to United States. And I was gung-ho that while I was not crazy in love with my husband, that I was determined. I knew that I didn't like many things about him. But I am going to change him. I'm going to make him happy. I'm going to make him act normal, not be-- and he couldn't talk anything about the past. But I was a survivor. I knew how to deal with all these things. Well, I arrived in the United States. And there were a lot of funny stories, I will tell you. Even going to the grocery store, I definitely bought Crisco to eat, because of the picture.  I mean, I had to read everything with the dictionary. But in three months, I learned English well enough to get a job. And I was already pregnant two months. And it was hard, because being pregnant, not having any friends, not having anybody to talk to, I felt very much alone. And even the best of husbands are not that great. [{PAUSES] I'm sorry. My husband, he would come home from work, and turn on television, watch sports and news. Well, I never even seen television before. In Israel, they didn't have any. So I thought all they had on television was sports and news. I was at home feeling very lonely, very homesick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, I'll turn on the television, see if they have sports and news. Well, they had which I didn't really know anything about it. But that was a movie. I could watch it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would write down words that I could understand, like I could hear many times. I did. And I-- then I would look them up at night in the dictionary. And I'd memorize them. But it was-- they talked very clearly. I didn't have to answer them. And it was interesting to watch. And that's the way I learned English. I got a job, as I said, after three months. I spoke well enough to get a job. And my-- my son was born April 15th. He's going to be-- he going to be 34 years old April 15th. Whoo. That was from-- it was a difficult time, because he was born with Cesarean section. It was a difficult pregnancy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somehow I managed. I mean, I was never really worried that I couldn't manage as a mother. My husband was always worried. How are we going to be parent? I said, nobody starts there. Everybody learns.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they all have parents. Somebody helps them. I said, well, somebody's going to help me. If I really need, I can ask neighbors. I am outgoing. And we bought a house when the son-- our son was about 10 months old. And we wanted a little girl. And when I was pregnant with my daughter, I realized that my husband had serious, serious problem, was that all my love and my caring-- I mean, I pampered the guy. I cut his toenails. I shampooed his hair. I was going to make him feel so good that he was going to be the happiest guy in the world. Didn't work. He developed severe problems. He had-- he couldn't take care of the boy. He was worried that he would-- there were a lot of sad things, like he worried that he was going to kill him or-- I don't know. Psychological problems. What really happened, the way I understand it, is that he all his life worried that he could not be a good father and a good husband, rather than thinking how a good husband he could be. And all that worry kicked in. And now he was worried that he's going to lose it all. And so he developed, in addition to that, he said he had cancer, that he was throwing up every morning. And I had an extremely difficult pregnancy. I had to be-- I had a tubal pregnancy between the two children. I lost one. And this one, I started the bleeding in the second month, so the doctor said, you have to be off your feet until the minimum of the seventh month. And we handled that, but I had here a husband who was sick. And the baby was born, again Cesarean, and little girl, 1963. And it was unbelievably difficult to me. I felt that I was overwhelmed with everything. And my husband said that he had cancer, and that he doesn't want to go to the hospital. So I forced him to go to the hospital. And they told him that the had a tiny little ulcer. So we cleared that problem. I think that all the trauma-- that he was in concentration camp four years. He actually grew up in concentration camp. The problem that I find with him, that he's not willing to face his problems. He's trying always to run away from them. Well the fact is, you cannot run away from your problems. You cannot run away from yourself. You have got to face your problems head on. And he has not learned that yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all my good will that I brought with me, I became extremely depressed after the second child was born. And by the time she was two years old and my son was four, I wanted to commit suicide-- because I wanted a family, and that was not a family.

speaker: interviewee
text: What really happened was I was driving around. I filed for divorce papers, because I said, I didn't want to live that way. But I had a nervous breakdown. My mind would go-- I would lose half a day. I wouldn't know where- I was with children in the car, and I didn't know what happened, and where they were, and why I was there, and how long I was there. And it really scared me to death. And-- because I didn't know how I was going to support them. And I felt that the least I owed my children was the security of a roof over their head, and food in their mouth, and somebody to care for them. So I knew that I couldn't go ahead with the divorce. I worked very hard at it myself. My husband never lifted a finger. I went to see a doctor. And I made myself get up every morning. I didn't want to get up. I would sit-- sometime it was noon before I would put on clothes. But little by little-- I think it took me two or three months to get out of this very, very deep depression. And the children were wonderful. They needed care, and they usually got a hold of me. And then I decided that I was going to work very hard to become independent so I could get a divorce and support the children. So that was my goal. I was going to earn a living somehow on my own. And I went back to work as a draftsman. And I worked full-time. Well, with a six-year-old and an eight-year-old, they came down with one little thing or another. And working mother, I couldn't be that super-mom.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I became ill. I had a gallbladder infection, and I needed surgery. So I realized that full-time job was very difficult for me to handle, but that I would work at it until I would somehow work it out. I worked at it until 1984. By 1984, I couldn't care less if I was going to be ever free and marry again, because I'm not interested in that whole issue.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this was basically the way I tried to make it. I wanted to help my children. It was very difficult. The other thing that came up, about the time when my son was six years old, the first time that little friends came to play tricks and treat on him. And they threw a bunch of corn at our window. I just came home from work. This was the job that I went back to to-- to try to get my life together again. And [{PAUSES] it threw me back. It threw me back. I mean, I almost froze in place. I knew I was in the United States. And I knew my home was my castle. And nobody could harm me. But I didn't understand why they had to do that. And so I [INAUDIBLE]. And the kids came back. And more kids came back. And it got-- and my husband said, it's all your fault. If you just ignored it, it-- they would go away. And I said, I cannot ignore it. I really cannot ignore it. And that went on from 1967 to 1979. [{PAUSES] It got very nasty. They would do it for at least a month, and around Halloween. They would do it on 4th of July. They would do it any time there was an excuse to harass me. Snow was falling. The snowballs were flying at my house the minute I entered the house. And one occasion I remember chasing one of the kids. And Alex, my son, came home on the school bus. He was a junior in high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he saw me chasing this kid. And he came, Mom, why can't you just be normal like everybody else? All of the kids on the bus were making fun of me, that my mother is crazy. [{PAUSES] And I remember getting in the car, because I had to go pick up my daughter from junior high school. And I was crying all the way there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I know that I haven't done anything wrong. I don't know what to do, where to turn. But if I could somehow tell my story, I'm sure that everybody would understand it, and they would stop-- the kids would stop doing the pranks and my son would even understand what-- But there was no information. Nobody talked about the Holocaust. There was very little statement about the Nazi concentration camps. And finally, in 1979, when the NBC aired-- they were advertising the docudrama-- I called the local station. And I said to them, I don't know if you have any documentary material. But I would be interested to see if you have any pictures from Auschwitz. I am very interested in seeing them. They said, well, no. No, we do not. It's basically mostly just a dramatized version of it. But since you are a survivor, would you mind appearing on the television on the news after that, just to give the local residents your view. I said, no, no problem. And so I appeared on the local television. And then they said, well, we have the last installment of the program. We would like you to come to the station so you would appear live on the program. And the children went with me. And they were doing their homework while the show was going on. And they thought that was kind of neat. And after that, people started asking me to lecture. I didn't really know very much about how to piece together-- what I remembered were bits and pieces of memories. I remembered the latrine. I remembered the pledge. I remembered the big rats. I remembered the potato story. I remember crawling in the bed, [INAUDIBLE]. I had to little by little piece it together. And they kept asking me more and more questions [INAUDIBLE] about details about the experiments. I said that's all I really knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the library and tried to read anything that had to do with Auschwitz. And I could find very little on the Mengele twins, very little. So I said, well, that is very strange. So ultimately, I came to-- and then I developed a need to verify, because there were many grownups who said, well, you probably don't remember. You were just a little child.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hated that patronizing, because there is-- there is a problem. Many grownups even today, survive, oh, you couldn't have possibly remembered it. You were just a child. I was 9 or 10 years old. I could definitely remember what I'm talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I felt that I needed to verify and to prove what I remembered was indeed so. So I said, well, maybe the only way for me to do that, find the other twins that I remember we were together in those pictures. I wrote to the museum. I got the film.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I wanted to know how I looked. I developed the film about 200 frames by frame. I asked the photographers to shoot and I was studying with magnifying glass for five months, until in one picture I recognize my sister. I did not recognize myself. I didn't know how I looked. [{PAUSES]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, of course, I recognize the Csengeri twins immediately, because I remember how they looked. Now I started wondering where they are, where, and how are they faring? And I said, wouldn't it be wonderful if I could locate them somewhere, and we could sit down? In my little naive mind, I said, we'll have a little gathering like a high school reunion, right? An Auschwitz reunion. And we could just discuss, share our different memories, and see what happened in the camp. Or do you remember when they blew up the camp? Do you remember the bombing? Do you remember different details? What experiments have they done on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that became a pretty big project. There was-- I didn't know. I wrote. I went first of all to reunions. And I went to Washington in '83. And I thought there would be a lot of twins. As I arrived there, I saw people carrying all kind of little signs. And I said, well, if I wanted to find somebody, I might as well make some kind of a little sign myself. And I had a package of extra pantyhose that I brought with me. So I ripped it open, I took all the cardboard from there, and I wrote 'Mengele twins, experiments, Birkenau' on one side. And the other side, I wrote 'Mozes, Port, [NON-ENGLISH] .' I said, anybody who's going to recognize the other side has to be very close relative or close neighbor from a nearby village. I was bombarded by questions about Mengele twins experiment. Nobody knew anything about it. I had to put that sign down. And I sat by a table, because I was just-- nobody knew anything about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I met one guy who says he was a twin. He knew something about it. And [{PAUSES] he-- we stayed in touch. And he said, well, [{PAUSES] why don't you put together a reunion? He said, I have been working on it for quite a few years. So I said to myself, this guy must have a list. I'm sure. I said, I'll help you a little bit. Sure. I called him. He said no, I don't have any list. I said, you mean you've been working on it for quite a few years, and you haven't found anybody, and you live in New York? He said no. I said, it couldn't be that difficult. So I started writing to NBC, CBS, magazine, all the biggies. I just went for the biggest, because they should help. My letter very simply stated. 50, 40 years ago, you didn't know who we were and what happened to us. Today, I'm trying to tell you that we are here, and I'm asking you to help us locate the other Mengele twins. Could you put in something, some little article in your newspaper, your magazine, your news release?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody answered. [{PAUSES] I was very disappointed. But I was-- I thought that I was on the right track, that I really wanted to see those twins. I wanted to really meet with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was washing the dishes in the kitchen and I was trying to figure out what could I do differently? And I said, hmm. Why don't I write to columnists? [{PAUSES] Art Buchwald writes about any idiotic thing. Jack Anderson writes about just about anything. They don't have too many people who will-- if-- if I can convince them, they will write it. I said, well, that's a good idea. Why didn't I think about it before that? Then I thought, well, I get very impressed when I remember getting a letter from Franklin Littell, who heads the Institute for Holocaust-- International Institute Holocaust something. He's the president. I thought, well, I'm impressed. I know that there isn't very much to it. Why don't I become-- put an organization, and put my name there as president? I'm just Eva Kor, nobody cares. But if I'm the president of some kind of an organization, that's a good way to do it. So I did that. I put together-- and I wrote my sister that I'm going to make her vice president for the Israeli survivors. And she said, don't put me in any kind of lectures. I don't speak. I don't want to lecture anywhere. I said, no, you just have to kind of help me find the twins in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what happened was that I happened to be working for the first time. I had a lot of trouble getting a job as a realtor, because I quit drafting, and I got my license as a realtor, and I started going to college to get my college degree. And in Terre Haute, Indiana, it's a very provincial in many ways. And they said, with an accent like you cannot sell real estate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I worked for a very small company, then I would earn like $2,000 a year. And at the time, the kids were in junior high and high school. So they needed clothes and everything else. And we were trying to save some money for college. So I had to work two other jobs to make the same money that I made as a draftsperson before that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I wanted to stay with it and I was not going to quit. I said, I am not going to ever quit real estate unless I am a success, because I don't ever want to look back at something that I did and look at it as a failure. And for six years, I was a complete failure. And then I kept knocking on the same doors. And finally, somebody said yes, big company-- the largest company in town. And now I had to prove myself. Because I heard the rumor that my boss said, she will never make it. And with all that pressure-- and at the same time, I started looking for the Mengele twins. And I made that organization. And now I was writing letters all kind of places. And I developed severe stomach trouble. [{PAUSES] And I wrote the letter after I found out that they were going to do a biopsy. And I started my letter very simply. It was a Friday when I found out that I would have to go Monday for a biopsy. I hope that the cruel hand of destiny will not again interfere in my normal course of life. But should that happen, and should that be my last request, this is what I would like you to do for me. I'm looking for the other Mengele twins. Please call me by my birthday, which is January 31st, or my liberation day, which is January 27. That had to be January 15 or something like that. And I described what the situation was. I got a telephone call January 29 from Jack Anderson, who was a reporter. At the same time, my sister was advertising in Israel. It was small ads in the paper. She didn't get anywhere, so I told her to contact my brother-in-law, who put in a ad, front page, center, Mengele twins who survived experiment looking for other Mengele twins for reunion. She got 80 telephone calls in one week. All of the media was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
